title: Heap (PQ) — ROI & Pattern
oneSentence: Always keep the top K items using a priority queue.
description: Master the Min-Heap pattern to solve Top K problems.

roi.signals: Top K, Kth Largest, Merge K Sorted.

sandbox.question: To find the Kth LARGEST, what type of heap do we use?
What is the time complexity of adding to a heap of size K?

followUps.q: What if you need top K from trillions of numbers in a stream?
followUps.a: Maintain a Min-Heap of exactly size K. For each new number, if it's larger than the heap's minimum (root), pop the root and push the new number. Space is strictly O(K).

followUps.q: How would you implement decrease-key or remove-arbitrary-element efficiently?
followUps.a: Standard PriorityQueue takes O(N) to find and remove an arbitrary element. You can maintain a separate HashMap mapping elements to their heap indices to achieve O(log N) removal, or use Java's TreeSet.

followUps.q: How to merge M sorted lists optimally using a heap?
followUps.a: Insert the head of each list into a Min-Heap. Pop the smallest, append to result, and insert the next element from the popped node's list. Time: O(N log M).

followUps.q: Min-Heap vs Max-Heap — when to use which?
followUps.a: Use a Min-Heap to find the Top K LARGEST elements (it evicts the smallest). Use a Max-Heap to find the Top K SMALLEST elements.

followUps.q: What's the time complexity of building a heap from N elements?
followUps.a: Inserting N elements one by one takes O(N log N). But using heapify (bottom-up), it takes exactly O(N) time.
